cb:div.p: Not of my stream, he pulls it up from the root completely. If this person goes forth, he will snatch away your broom handle.

cb:div.head: Shide執經卷
cb:div.p: Asked about his surname, and he does not know it; asked his given name, and he does not recognize it. Fully holding up a single scripture scroll, how does that compare to standing with hands folded?

cb:div.cb:mulu: The Fifth Patriarch sends the Sixth Patriarch across the river
cb:div.head: The Fifth Patriarch sends the Sixth Patriarch across the river
cb:div.p:
He was only a rice-pounder on long duty, yet he overturned old conventions with life as fragile as thread. A small boat, one night, rows through the bright moon.
Only when raising a child does one know a father’s kindness.

cb:div.head: The boatman
cb:div.p: Leaning on the orchid oar, he sits, his two eyes empty and vast. Coldly he gazes at the red-weed shore, his intent resting on the dragon in the blue pool.

cb:div.head: Yanyang
cb:div.p: Bringing not a single thing, yet carrying it all on his shoulders. This poisonous mind—could it be a fierce tiger?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Venerable Zhen, exhumation and return of remains to Quanzhou
cb:div.head: Venerable Zhen, exhumation and return of remains to Quanzhou
cb:div.p:
Sound and form are utterly pure; seeing and hearing are unobstructed. If it can be spoken, then it stops there, only slightly revealing half. With a sweep of the sleeve, he departs,
still lacking one final turn. Hūng hūng—at Ling Mountain’s gate, there is still no place for you.

cb:div.head: Exhumation of Yanjing Head
cb:div.p:
At the eastern privy head, in Yanshou, relieving oneself on the clean ground—already seen before the skull. If one goes on like this,
with no final resting place, what then in the end? My home is in Eastern Prefecture.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Fire sermon for Yizang, Scripture Librarian (formerly chanter at Jingshan)
cb:div.head: Fire sermon for Yizang曾在徑山維那
cb:div.p:
Raising the torch, he says: this meaning is profound and subtle, beyond anyone’s reach. He once personally contended in Dharma battle with seventeen hundred people,
all of whom listened to the full presentation. In reply to the monk asking about letters, the entire Tripiṭaka is not worth a single spit—lift your gaze to Yunyan.
Yun on the cliff says: coral branches, branch by branch, day after day. Yizang, do you see it? Do not call it fire.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Sealing the niche of Layman Yong
cb:div.head: Sealing the niche of Layman Yong
cb:div.p:
This pinch of that person is all iron. In death one sees action; once attained, eternally attained. The gate is shut on the clear wind
□□ in leisure, scattered flower rain lies in utter disarray.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Entering the stupa of Record Keeper Zun
cb:div.head: Entering the stupa of Record Keeper Zun
cb:div.p:
Using Huanglong’s single device, following the ritual of the Seven Buddhas. Not adding a single embellishment, his whole body is raw and unadorned strength.
How high is heaven, how extreme is earth—through iron walls and silver mountains one enters from here.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Offering tea for West Hall monk Bu Tuoji
cb:div.head: Offering tea for West Hall monk Bu Tuoji
cb:div.p:
So-and-so, a spiritual sprout of Eastern Ravine, with the true character of the monastic forest. Before Jade Desk Peak, sharing the seat and upholding teaching. At Mount Putuo,
by the cliffside, cloud waves roll like snow. He swept away the five flavors of Chan in all directions, and exchanged the tongues of monks for the three talents.
Farewell—drink tea, treasure it, and rest.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Fire sermon for Record Keeper Ziming (temporarily arrived at death)
cb:div.head: Fire sermon for Ziming, Record Keeper暫到死

cb:div.cb:mulu: Fire sermon for Zhidianza, Head Cook
cb:div.head: Fire sermon for Zhidianza, Head Cook
cb:div.p:
His aspiration is admirable, and his inclination is also admirable. He speaks of ginger yet does not know its spiciness, and again says black beans…
Good for making sauce. With a sudden turn of the mechanism, both mouth and ears are lost. How is it that in the various places they are buried alive—here
cremation.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Torch for West Hall monk Konghai Bao
cb:div.head: Torch for West Hall monk Konghai Bao
cb:div.p:
Before the mechanism of the sleeping tiger, in the depths where the dragon is trapped, smashing the clay basin and breaking the black bamboo staff—this is Konghai
who has the eyes of one who has traveled widely and fully investigated everywhere. Beneath Nanping Peak, before the Hall of Right Reception, occupying the seat of Nanquan,
carrying Muzhou’s wooden board—this is Konghai’s function of sharing the seat and presenting the essential teaching. In the furnace of smelting gold, using
the method of □clear sky, showing the sickness of sentient beings, folding the tongue of Vimalakīrti—again Konghai enters into descending-hand □
□□. As for the illness of being unable to gather it together, and precisely the inability to find where it arises—how does Konghai
theorize it? He throws down the fire brand and says: at this moment the liver and gall both split apart, like water with water, fire with
fire.

cb:div.cb:mulu: For shaving the novice’s head and bestowing the robe
cb:div.head: For shaving the novice’s head and bestowing the robe
cb:div.p: At the highest point of the mechanism, it is clearly revealed. Without moving the razor, the hair and beard fall of themselves.
Buddha hands it to Buddha, patriarchs transmit it to patriarchs. No trace of the thread is revealed—the stupa rests on the left shoulder.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Written by Head Seat Qian after the general teaching of Master Songyuan
cb:div.head: Written by Head Seat Qian after the general teaching of Master Songyuan
cb:div.p:
When illness is many, one becomes familiar with medicinal properties; only after proven efficacy is a prescription transmitted. Master Songyuan once served Master Yingan at
Zhongshan, and obtained this effective prescription, widely applying it to cure the illnesses of the many. Elder Xun, Head Seat Qian, selected
responses, harmonized and wrote them down, making them even more manifest. As it is said, curses and all poisonous medicines return to their originator,
so one must be very careful.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded Sayings of Venerable Yungu, Scroll Lower終

cb:div.head: No. 1454-A
cb:div.p:
In the Dharma talks at Nantang, sometimes Guanyiu’s “Mountain Dwelling” poems are recited, or Liu Qiqing’s lyrics are sung—some say this is not
Chan—can that be so? In recent times there is a fondness for strange and affected styles; if one’s insight does not reach the ancients, it is like actors in a theater
performing history, speaking of matters resembling Liu Bang and Xiang Yu—those who take it in do not tire. How is this not true history
itself? If one has realization, even without such records, who does not have such records? If there is no realization, even if there are such records,
who truly possesses them? Someone says: your argument is too high—then there would be no recorded sayings in the world. Yungu looks
upon scholars—how could one make them without transmission? I folded my hands and apologized, saying: I have offended, I have offended. In the year Wuchen
written on the first day of the ninth month.

p.note: 輕字原本不明或怪字乎
餕餡【CB】，酸饀【卍續】（CBETA 按：「餕」音 jùn，不通「酸」(suān)。「饀」音 tāo，通「饕」(tāo)，非「餡」(xiàn)之異體字。）
餡【CB】，饀【卍續】（CBETA 按：「饀」音 tāo，通「饕」(tāo)，非「餡」(xiàn)之異體字。）
